project.objective: Did Stephen Joseph Twohig have any children beyond the two already linked in the FamilySearch tree (Genevieve "Jennie" Ballard and Robert P. Twohig)?

questions.question: What does the 1900 U.S. federal census show about Stephen Joseph Twohig's household composition and children in the Bronx, New York?
questions.rationale: The 1900 census is the most important single record for a completeness question about children. It would capture all living children in the household ca. 1900, and the census also records the mother's fertility count (number of children born vs. number living). Stephen (b. 1857) would be ~43; Jennie (b. ~1886) and Robert (b. ~1889) would both be teenagers still at home. Any additional children born between 1880–1900 would appear here if still living. This census has not yet been examined. It is the natural gatekeeper record: its household roster and mother's fertility count together set the baseline for how many children we need to account for.

exhaustive_declaration.justification: Record type breadth insufficient: no NYC birth registrations (1884–1889), no Catholic church/baptism records, and the 1910 census (already in tree as 7964-Z1W) not extracted. Overturn risk elevated — NYC birth records for the period are a real, searchable category and could reveal additional children who died before 1900 or a different father. GJGC-RJZ's absence from both the 1900 and 1905 household censuses is unexplained. Two threshold questions fail: (1) broad range of record types not yet covered; (2) overturn risk is real and concrete.

stop_criteria.goal_alignment: Partially met. Two censuses consistently show 3 children (Janette b.1884, Florence b.1886, Robert b.1889); no census fertility count available (Stephen was widowed). The completeness objective — whether other children existed — cannot be answered without birth/church records.
stop_criteria.repository_breadth: Not met. Census records (1880–1905, 5 attempts) and one vital record (Stephen's death certificate). NYC birth registrations (1884–1889), Catholic baptism registers, and the 1910 census extract are absent. All three are realistic and searchable for this population.
stop_criteria.original_substitution: Partially met. 1900 and 1905 censuses accessed via FamilySearch record_read (derivative indexed GEDCOMX). Death record also derivative. Original schedule images not consulted, which is acceptable for positive household records but less defensible for a completeness claim.
stop_criteria.independent_verification: Partially met. The 1900 US census and 1905 NY State Census are two independent sources with different enumerators and different collection origins, both showing the same 3 children. Independence is genuine. Limitation: neither census captures children who died or left home before 1900.
stop_criteria.evidence_class: Partially met. 1900 federal census is an original-class record for household composition. No original-source birth record (civil registration or baptismal) has been examined for any of the three identified children.
stop_criteria.conflict_resolution: Mostly met. c_001 (Florence Twohig ≠ GJGC-RJZ) resolved by same_person score 0.003 and different married names. c_002 (Jennie L = Janette = I1) resolved. Unexplained anomaly: GJGC-RJZ absent from both 1900 and 1905 household censuses despite being ~14/~19 at those dates; her relationship to Stephen is assumed from the FS tree but not independently verified in this project.
stop_criteria.overturn_risk: Elevated. NYC civil birth registration was active 1884–1889. A search of those records could reveal (a) additional children who died before 1900 census age, (b) a different father named on a birth record, or (c) GJGC-RJZ appearing under her birth name with Stephen listed as father. These are concrete, searchable outcomes — not speculative.

items.jurisdiction: Bronx, New York City, New York, United States
items.rationale: The 1900 U.S. federal census is the highest-priority record for this completeness question. It would enumerate every person in Stephen's household ca. June 1900, including all living children, and critically records the mother's fertility count (number of children born vs. number living), which sets a floor on how many children must be accounted for. Stephen (b. 1857, ~43) and Jennie (b. ~1886) and Robert (b. ~1889) should all appear; any additional children born before 1900 who were still living would also be listed. FamilySearch has the 1900 census indexed.

items.jurisdiction: Bronx, New York City, New York, United States
items.rationale: Ancestry also indexes the 1900 U.S. federal census with its own OCR/transcription pass. If FamilySearch returns no match (possible under variant spellings — Twohig, Twohy, Twoig, Toohig, Toohey), Ancestry's index may catch a variant not indexed on FamilySearch.

items.jurisdiction: New York City, New York, United States
items.rationale: The 1890 U.S. federal census was almost entirely destroyed in a 1921 fire. The New York State Census of 1892 (FamilySearch collection 1529100) is the best available substitute for New York residents at this period. In 1892 Jennie would be ~6 and Robert ~3 — both should appear. Any additional children born before 1892 and still living would also appear. This census bridges the critical 1890 gap.

items.jurisdiction: New York City, New York, United States
items.rationale: The New York State Census of 1905 (FamilySearch collection 1463113) captures household composition five years after the federal census, between the 1900 and 1910 enumerations. Any children born after 1900 or children who had left home by 1910 but were still at home in 1905 would appear here. The 1910 census source in the tree (7964-Z1W) has not yet been extracted; the 1905 state census is a distinct source offering an independent enumeration.

items.jurisdiction: Bronx, New York City, New York, United States
items.rationale: The 1880 U.S. federal census shows Stephen at approximately age 23. He may already have been married — Jennie was born ~1886, but an earlier marriage and earlier children are possible. This census would establish who Stephen was living with and whether any children pre-date Jennie. The Bronx (West Farms area) is known from the 1870 record, so a search in Westchester/New York should locate him.

items.jurisdiction: New York City, New York, United States
items.rationale: Stephen J. Twohig's death record (SVMY-C2J, New York City Municipal Deaths, 18 Jan 1920) is already in the tree but has not been extracted as research assertions. The informant on this certificate is likely one of Stephen's surviving children, which would help confirm which children were alive and recognized at his death. This record corroborates Stephen's identity and may name surviving children or the informant's relationship to the deceased.

items.jurisdiction: New York City, New York, United States
items.rationale: New York City civil birth registration was active for all three identified children's birth years (Janette b.Aug 1884, Florence b.Jun 1886, Robert b.Mar 1889). Birth records would name both parents (confirming Stephen as father and identifying the mother), and a search of the collection could reveal additional registered children not appearing in later censuses because they died in infancy or emigrated.

items.jurisdiction: Bronx, New York City, New York, United States
items.rationale: The 1910 census source (7964-Z1W, ark:/61903/1:1:M5HH-9QF) is already attached to the FS tree and records the Florence Feurfile household — Florence Twohig after her marriage to George Feurfile. Extracting this record via record_read confirms Florence's identity as Stephen's daughter (now in her husband's household), corroborates the post-1905 trajectory, and may reveal other household members. A separate search for Stephen himself in 1910 would show his final household before his Jan 1920 death.

items.jurisdiction: Bronx, New York City, New York, United States
items.rationale: GJGC-RJZ (Genevieve 'Jennie' Ballard, b.1886 Bronx) is linked in the FS tree as Stephen's daughter but was absent from his household in both the 1900 and 1905 censuses. A search of the 1900 census under her birth surname (Twohig, b.~1886 female, Bronx/NY) or a search for a female Twohig boarder/servant in NYC would either locate her in another household (explaining the absence) or return nil (deepening the anomaly). This directly bears on the completeness count.

log.notes: Strong match (score 0.855): 'Household of Steven Twohig, 1900' (ark:/61903/1:1:MS2W-GPL), Bronx, NY. Household shows 3 children: Janette Twohig (b. Aug 1884, F), Florence Twohig (b. Jun 1886, F), Robert Twohig (b. Mar 1889, M), plus an unattached unknown 5th person (ark:/61903/1:1:62YQ-ZQP5). Tree currently links only 2 children (Genevieve/Jennie b.~1886, Robert b.~1889). Janette (b.1884) appears to be a previously unrecognized child. Stephen listed as widowed in 1900. Marriage fact on record dated 1884. Record attached in FS tree to KCF4-NQ3 (not GQV2-NKK) — possible duplicate tree profile. 2nd result was a city directory (1899-1900), not a census.
Zero results for Stephen Twohig in NY State Census 1892 (collection 1529100) with exact parameters.
Zero results for variant spellings Toohig and Twohy + Stephen in 1892 NY census. Neither variant found Stephen in New York.

Coverage check: 36 Twohig records exist in collection 1529100, all from Brooklyn (Kings County) — zero from the Bronx or New York City proper. The 1892 NY State Census on FamilySearch does not appear to cover the Bronx district. Stephen Twohig's household in the Bronx is not indexed in this collection. Absence is a collection-coverage gap, not evidence of non-residence. The Bronx portion of the 1892 NY State Census may be on Ancestry or undigitized.

Strong match: 'Household of Stephen W Twohig, 1905 NY State Census' (ark:/61903/1:1:SP6F-ZXY), Bronx, AD 35, ED 50. Children: Jennie L (b.1884 F), Florence (b.1886 F), Robert (b.1889 M). Janette from 1900 census (b. Aug 1884) appears to now go by 'Jennie L' — same birth year. GJGC-RJZ (Genevieve 'Jennie' Ballard, b.1886) remains absent. Florence still in household (marries Feurfile between 1905-1910 per existing tree source 7964-Z1W). treeMatches for Stephen: KCF4-NQ3 (5 stars), KCVD-9WP (4 stars), GQV2-NKK (0 stars) — possible duplicate tree issue.
Collection mismatch: all 3 results are from US City and Business Directories (collection 3754697), not the 1880 census. None are relevant. Will retry with specific 1880 census collection ID.

Zero results for Stephen Twohig in 1880 US Census collection (1417683) with NY residence. Also tried Patrick Twohig (b.1819, father) - returned only a phonetic mis-match (Edwin Twiggs). Suggests the Twohig family may be indexed under a spelling variant in this collection.
Zero results — variant spelling Toohig + Stephen in 1880 census (collection 1417683) with NY residence filter.
Zero results — searching for father Patrick Twohig (b.1819 Cork) as head of household in 1880 census (collection 1417683) with NY residence filter.
Zero results — variant spelling Twohy + Stephen in 1880 census with NY residence filter.
Zero results — variant spelling Toohig for father Patrick in 1880 census with NY filter.

4 results returned without NY geographic filter, but none match Stephen Twohig of Bronx: results are in Maryland (Twigg), West Virginia (Twohig), Vermont (Twigg), and California (Twiligan). The Twohig family does not appear in the 1880 census index for any NY location under this spelling.
Zero results for Patrick Twohig (b.1819) without geographic filter in 1880 census. Confirms surname 'Twohig' for Patrick is not indexed in this collection.

3 results for Tuohy + Stephen, none in NY: Nebraska (Touhey, b.1855 NY — different family), California (Chinese miner — no match), Illinois (Tuohey — no match). Irish variant 'Tuohy' also fails to locate Stephen in NY.

6 results for Tuohy + Patrick (b.1814-1824), none match Patrick Twohig of Bronx: Flushing Queens NY (b.1817, different family/location), Bradford PA (b.1819 — birth year matches but wife is Mary b.1829 not Mary b.1826 O'Connell, and in Pennsylvania), Minnesota, Boston MA, Schuyler County NY, Lowell MA. After 9+ FamilySearch variants (Twohig, Toohig, Twohy, Tuohy; Stephen and Patrick; with and without NY filter), the Twohig family from West Farms/Bronx is not locatable in FamilySearch's 1880 census index. Probable cause: indexing gap or highly unusual transcription of the surname in the original schedule.

Death record for Stephen J. Twohig, 18 Jan 1920, Bronx NY. Confirms: b.8 Feb 1857, widowed, occupation Builder, parents Patrick Twohig (b. Ireland) and Mary O'Connell (b. Ireland). No surviving children are named in the indexed record. Record corroborates Stephen's identity and parentage.

person_evidence.rationale: Census head 'Steven Twohig': surname exact match; given name Steven/Stephen variant; birth Feb 1856 vs. 8 Feb 1857 in tree (1-year difference, typical census rounding); widowed status consistent; Bronx NY location consistent with tree. same_person scored 0.855 in search-records. Strong match on name, approximate birth year, and location.

Birth date 'February 1856' from 1900 census head persona. GQV2-NKK known birth 8 Feb 1857 — 1-year discrepancy; census birth year reporting is notoriously imprecise. Supported by strong name and location match.
Birth place 'New York' from 1900 census head. GQV2-NKK tree shows 'U.S.' — consistent; the 1910 and 1870 census sources already attached to GQV2-NKK also say New York. No conflict.
Census residence The Bronx, New York City, 1900. Consistent with GQV2-NKK's known 1910 Bronx residence and 1920 Bronx death.
Marital status 'Widowed' in 1900 census. Tree shows GQV2-NKK's marital status as Widowed. Consistent with wife being absent/deceased.
Marriage year ~1884 derived from 'years married' column. GQV2-NKK is the head; this bears on his marriage history. No independent corroboration of marriage year yet.

Census child_1 'Janette Twohig' (F, b. Aug 1884): name exact match to stub I1 (Janette Twohig); gender match; listed as daughter of Steven Twohig (GQV2-NKK), who is also I1's parent in tree. matchRelatives score 0.995. Confidence probable per stub rule — I1 rests on this single record with no independent corroboration yet. Note: stub was created from this same census; high score reflects name identity, not independent confirmation.
Birth date 'August 1884' for census child Janette. Links to I1 (Janette Twohig stub). No independent corroboration yet.
Birth place 'New York' for census child Janette. Links to I1 (Janette Twohig stub).
Residence The Bronx 1900 for census child Janette. Links to I1 (Janette Twohig stub).
Relationship assertion 'daughter of head of household Steven Twohig': child side. Links to I1 (Janette) as the daughter.

Relationship assertion 'daughter of head of household Steven Twohig': parent side. Links to GQV2-NKK as the head/father. Steven Twohig in this record is confidently identified as GQV2-NKK (same_person 0.855).
Marital status 'Single' for census child Janette. Links to I1.

Census child_2 'Florence Twohig' (F, b. Jun 1886): name exact match to stub I2 (Florence Twohig); gender match; listed as daughter of Steven Twohig (GQV2-NKK). matchRelatives score 0.995. Confidence probable per stub rule. Direct same_person comparison with GJGC-RJZ (Genevieve 'Jennie' Ballard, b.1886) scored 0.003 (no match) — Florence and Jennie treated as distinct individuals. GJGC-RJZ was not paired with any 1900 census persona by matchRelatives, and is absent from this census — potential conflict to investigate.
Birth date 'June 1886' for census child Florence. Links to I2 (Florence Twohig stub).
Birth place 'New York' for census child Florence. Links to I2.
Residence The Bronx 1900 for census child Florence. Links to I2.

Relationship assertion 'daughter of head of household Steven Twohig': parent side. Links to GQV2-NKK as the father.
Marital status 'Single' for census child Florence. Links to I2.

Census child_3 'Robert Twohig' (M, b. Mar 1889): surname exact match; given name Robert match; birth Mar 1889 consistent with tree birth year 1889; Bronx NY location consistent; GT51-LBD already linked as child of GQV2-NKK (Stephen) in tree, consistent with census parent-child relationship. matchRelatives score 0.995. Strong match on name, birth year, location, and family context.
Birth date 'March 1889' for census child Robert. Consistent with GT51-LBD tree birth year 1889.
Birth place 'New York' for census child Robert. Consistent with GT51-LBD tree birth place Bronx, NY.
Residence The Bronx 1900 for census child Robert. Consistent with GT51-LBD known Bronx residence.
Relationship 'son of head of household Steven Twohig': child side. Links to GT51-LBD (Robert), who is already linked in tree as Stephen's child.

Marital status 'Single' for census child Robert. Links to GT51-LBD.

Name 'Stephen W Twohig' matches GQV2-NKK (Stephen Joseph Twohig). Middle initial 'W' unknown but not conflicting. Location Bronx 1905 consistent with GQV2-NKK's known Bronx residences. record_read source; no same_person score available; correlation analysis supports confident identification.

Birth year 1858 vs GQV2-NKK known birth 8 Feb 1857 — 1-year discrepancy, typical census rounding. Corroborated by 1900 census birth year of 1856 (same 1-year range). Supported by name and location match.
Bronx, AD 35, ED 50 residence in 1905 consistent with GQV2-NKK's known Bronx residences in 1900, 1910, 1920 (death).

Name 'Jennie L Twohig' (F, b.1884): birth year 1884 matches I1 (Janette Twohig, b. Aug 1884 per 1900 census) exactly. 'Jennie' is a documented American English diminutive/variant of 'Janet/Janette' (both derive from the Jane name family). Same father GQV2-NKK confirmed. Alternative candidate: GJGC-RJZ (Genevieve 'Jennie' Ballard, b.1886) — shares the nickname 'Jennie' but birth year 1886 conflicts with this record's 1884 by 2 years. Birth year exact match favors I1; kept probable because name shift Janette→Jennie L is unexplained without further evidence.
Birth year 1884 corroborates I1 (Janette Twohig, b. Aug 1884). Two independent census sources (1900 and 1905) now both show a daughter of Stephen born 1884 in his Bronx household.
Bronx 1905 residence for child_1 links to I1 (Janette/Jennie L Twohig).

conflicts.description: The 1900 U.S. census shows a daughter 'Florence Twohig' (b. Jun 1886, Bronx) in Stephen Twohig's household (a_013). The FamilySearch tree already contains GJGC-RJZ (Genevieve 'Jennie' Ballard, b. 1886, Bronx) as Stephen's daughter, linked via rel-4. GJGC-RJZ is entirely absent from the 1900 census household, though she would have been ~14. The identity question is whether Florence Twohig (I2) and GJGC-RJZ are the same individual or two distinct daughters.
conflicts.identity_question: Is 'Florence Twohig' (child_2 in the 1900 U.S. census, b. Jun 1886, Bronx, linked as I2) the same person as GJGC-RJZ (Genevieve 'Jennie' Ballard, b. 1886, Bronx, already linked as Stephen Twohig's daughter via rel-4)?
conflicts.independence_analysis: The evidence bearing on this question comes from two independent source streams. First, the 1900 U.S. census indexed entry (src_001, derivative of the original schedule) establishes 'Florence Twohig' as a daughter in Stephen's household — a_013 through a_018 capture her name, birth, residence, and relationship. Second, GJGC-RJZ's profile in the FamilySearch tree derives from separate original records: a 1910 census appearance (source 7964-Z1W, showing 'Florence E Feurfile' — a different individual — in a separate household), a 1920 census entry (source 7964-HG8, showing William Ballard and James Ballard — consistent with Jennie having married into the Ballard family), and other residences (1886–1940). These two source streams are genuinely independent: neither is derived from the other, and the FamilySearch same_person comparison between the two personas (p_64982513 and GJGC-RJZ) scored 0.003, reflecting the tool's own assessment that they are informationally distinct. The two source streams therefore carry independent evidential weight, and no single informant can have produced both.
conflicts.weighing_analysis: Three factors decisively favor treating Florence and Jennie as different people. (1) Given-name discrepancy: 'Florence' and 'Genevieve/Jennie' share no phonetic overlap, no common nickname, and no documented variant relationship — this is not the kind of name variation that arises from indexer error or regional custom. (2) same_person algorithm: the FamilySearch comparison of the two personas returned 0.003, functionally zero — the algorithm found virtually no similarity in name, date, or place data. (3) The 1910 census source already in the tree (7964-Z1W, titled 'Stephen J Twoking, United States, Census, 1910') has its citation entry pointing to the household of George E Feurfile and Florence E Feurfile — strongly indicating that Florence Twohig married George Feurfile before 1910 and appears in the 1910 census under that surname, as a separately enumerated household. GJGC-RJZ, by contrast, appears with the surname Ballard (from marriage to a Ballard), has a 1910 residence in the Bronx, and is listed with Marital Status: Married — a trajectory entirely independent of the Feurfile marriage. The only factor shared by Florence and Jennie is the birth year 1886, which is insufficient for identity where names are incompatible.
conflicts.resolution_rationale: The conflict concerns whether the 1900 census child_2 'Florence Twohig' (I2) is the same person as GJGC-RJZ (Genevieve 'Jennie' Ballard). The 1900 census clearly names this child 'Florence'; the tree person carries the given name 'Genevieve' with nickname 'Jennie' — there is no documented pathway between those names. The same_person comparison scored 0.003, reflecting near-total informational non-overlap. The 1910 census source already attached to the Stephen Twohig tree profile (7964-Z1W) describes a household of 'George E Feurfile and Florence E Feurfile,' providing affirmative corroboration that Florence Twohig became Florence Feurfile by 1910 — a distinct life trajectory from GJGC-RJZ, who became Jennie Ballard. These are almost certainly two different daughters of Stephen Twohig, both born ca. 1886 (or with the tree birth year for Jennie being slightly off). GJGC-RJZ's complete absence from the 1900 census is the residual unexplained element. Possible explanations include: (a) Jennie was living away from home in June 1900 — working as a domestic servant, boarding, or staying with relatives — which was common for adolescent girls in this period; (b) she was enumerated elsewhere in New York but not yet connected to this family; or (c) the enumerator missed or skipped her. The conflict cannot be marked 'resolved' under the schema because the conclusion is that these are different people (no preferred assertion among the competing set). A targeted 1900 census search for Genevieve or Jennie Twohig in New York would either locate her in a separate enumeration or, if absent, provide negative evidence consistent with being away from home.

conflicts.description: The 1905 NY State Census shows 'Jennie L Twohig' (F, b.1884, child_1 of Stephen W Twohig) as assertion a_028. Two candidate identities compete: (A) I1 = Janette Twohig (b. Aug 1884), created from the 1900 census 'Janette Twohig' who also appears as child_1 with exact birth year 1884; (B) GJGC-RJZ = Genevieve 'Jennie' Ballard (b.1886), whose given nickname 'Jennie' matches the 1905 name but whose tree birth year of 1886 conflicts by 2 years.
conflicts.identity_question: Is 'Jennie L Twohig' (child_1, 1905 NY State Census, b.1884) the same person as I1 (Janette Twohig, b. Aug 1884 per 1900 census) or GJGC-RJZ (Genevieve 'Jennie' Ballard, b.1886)?
conflicts.independence_analysis: Three independent sources bear on this question. First, the 1900 U.S. census (src_001, derivative indexed) establishes 'Janette Twohig' (F, b. Aug 1884) as child_1 in Stephen's household. Second, the 1905 NY State Census (src_002, derivative indexed) establishes 'Jennie L Twohig' (F, b. 1884) as child_1 in the same household. These two census sources are genuinely independent: different original schedules, different indexing passes, five years apart. Third, the FamilySearch tree profile for GJGC-RJZ (Genevieve 'Jennie' Ballard, b.1886) derives from entirely different records (1910 and 1920 censuses, later residence records) and is independent of both censuses. The matchRelatives algorithm paired 'Jennie L' with GJGC-RJZ based on name similarity, but that pairing is an algorithmic heuristic input — it does not constitute independent corroboration.
conflicts.weighing_analysis: Three factors decisively favor interpreting 'Jennie L Twohig' (1905) as I1 (Janette, b. Aug 1884). (1) Birth year: 1884 matches I1 exactly across two independent censuses (1900 and 1905), while GJGC-RJZ's tree birth year of 1886 conflicts by 2 full years. Census birth year is often off by a year due to age rounding, but a 2-year discrepancy across two records from two different census years is unlikely to be a shared coincidental error. (2) Household continuity: the same household enumerated in 1900 shows 'Janette' (b.1884) as the eldest daughter, and in 1905 shows 'Jennie L' (b.1884) as the eldest daughter. Janette/Janet→Jennie is a well-documented American English nickname equivalence (both derivatives of the Jane name family). A child called 'Janette' at age 15 going by 'Jennie' at age 21 is entirely normal. (3) GJGC-RJZ's persistent absence: GJGC-RJZ is absent from both the 1900 and 1905 censuses. If she were the 'Jennie L' of 1905, she would have been in the household in 1905 but inexplicably absent in 1900 at age 14 — and her birth year in the tree (1886) would need to be corrected to 1884.
conflicts.resolution_rationale: The conflict is whether 'Jennie L Twohig' (1905 census, b.1884) is I1 (Janette Twohig, b. Aug 1884) or GJGC-RJZ (Genevieve 'Jennie' Ballard, b.1886). The 1900 and 1905 censuses, taken as a pair, place a daughter of Stephen born 1884 as the eldest child in both enumerations — first as 'Janette,' then as 'Jennie L.' The birth year is identical across both records, and the names are variants of the same Jane/Janet family. This coheres into a single person using a slightly different name form. GJGC-RJZ, by contrast, has a tree birth year of 1886 that conflicts with both census records' 1884 figure. The matchRelatives algorithm's pairing of 'Jennie L' with GJGC-RJZ reflects name-based scoring and cannot override the birth year evidence from two independent sources. The less-reliable claim — that GJGC-RJZ was born 1886 — most likely derives from a single undated tree entry and the 1910 census (where age-reporting errors of 2 years are not unusual). An American woman named 'Genevieve' using the nickname 'Jennie' is well-documented; the same applies to 'Janette' using 'Jennie' informally. Based on birth year exact match across two independent censuses and household continuity, 'Jennie L Twohig' (1905) is identified as I1 (Janette Twohig). GJGC-RJZ's absence from both the 1900 and 1905 Stephen Twohig household censuses remains unexplained and warrants further investigation in q_001-related follow-up.
